content.problemStatement:
You are given an integer NNN. You need to find an N×NN\times NN×N matrix such that:


Each element of the matrix is an integer from 111 to N2N^2N2;
All elements of the matrix are **unique**;
The absolute difference between elements in neighbouring cells is **strictly greater** than 111.
It can be shown that there is at least one matrix that satisfies the given conditions. If multiple matrices satisfy the given conditions, print any.

Note that two cells are considered to be neighbours if they have a common side **or** a common vertex (i.e. a cell can have at most 888 neighbouring cells).


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case contains a single line of input, an integer NNN, denoting the number of rows and columns in the required matrix.


Output Format
For each test case, output NNN lines, where the ithi^{th}ith line contains NNN space-separated integers, denoting the elements of the ithi^{th}ith row.
constraints: Sum of N^2 over all test cases does not exceed 10^6
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single integer N (the size of the required square matrix).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output N lines. Each line contains N space‑separated integers – the rows of the constructed matrix. Print a newline after each test case (optional).

sampleTestCases.explanation: Numbers 1..16 are placed with all odds first then evens. Any two neighbouring cells differ by at least 2, satisfying the required condition.
For N=5 the matrix contains odds 1..25 followed by evens 2..24. For N=6 the same rule is applied up to 36.

hidden.label: maximum boundary (N=1000, single test case)
hidden.expectedOutput: The first line contains numbers 1 3 5 ... 999 (500 numbers). Subsequent lines follow the same odd‑then‑even row‑major order up to 1000000. (Exact matrix omitted for brevity but follows the described construction.)

solution.approach: We need a permutation of numbers 1 … N^2 such that any two cells sharing a side or a corner differ by more than 1. A simple construction works: list all odd numbers in increasing order, then all even numbers in increasing order, and fill the matrix row‑by‑row with this sequence. All odd numbers differ from each other by at least 2, the same holds for evens. Any odd is at least 3 larger than the smallest even (2), and any even is at most N^2‑2 smaller than the largest odd (N^2‑1). Because the whole odd block appears before the even block, the only possible odd‑even neighbours are at the border between the two blocks, where the odd value is large and the even value is small, so their difference is far greater than 1. Hence the condition is satisfied for every neighbour (including diagonal ones). The algorithm runs in O(N^2) time and O(1) extra space per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N^2) per test case (linear in the size of the matrix).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) additional space besides the output matrix.

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it))
        total = N * N
        seq = list(range(1, total + 1, 2)) + list(range(2, total + 1, 2))
        idx = 0
        for r in range(N):
            row = seq[idx:idx+N]
            out_lines.append(' '.join(map(str, row)))
            idx += N
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
